subject_profile.summary: Base country-level assessment; default category weights prioritize rule of law, institutional integrity, and personal freedom.
stated_values: Rule of law and institutional integrity weighted most heavily
Personal freedom and security from arbitrary state action are foundational
Economic success is contingent on rule of law, not a substitute for it

flags: Generated 2026-05-28 under v2.0: multilateral + independent (IMF, World Bank, ADB, USIP, Freedom House, TI, Lowy) + international press; state media EXCLUDED on governance.

A geopolitical flashpoint that swung back West - the 2019 Taiwan-to-China switch and 2022 China security pact made it a contest zone; on 7 May 2026 PM Manele was ousted and pact-critic Matthew Wale became PM after the courts forced the vote (very recent, trajectory uncertain, reduced confidence).

Standout drags: aid-dependence and a depleting logging base, the Malaita/Guadalcanal 'Tensions' legacy and 2021 anti-China Honiara riots, weak health/infrastructure, and acute climate vulnerability (low-lying islands disappearing).

economic.notes: Low debt but a deteriorating, aid-dependent fiscal trajectory on a depleting logging base.

fiscal_state.notes: Persistent ~3% deficits and depleted cash reserves; public debt roughly tripled since 2019 (to ~20% of GDP) as logging revenue declines - low level but a deteriorating trajectory. (IMF 2024 Art IV)

monetary_independence.notes: The CBSI runs a basket peg of the SBD providing nominal stability, but policy space is minimal in an aid/import-dependent economy. (IMF 2024)

reserve_currency_intl_monetary.notes: The SBD is not a reserve currency. (definitional)

inflation.notes: Moderate and broadly contained, though import-price-sensitive. (IMF 2024; World Bank MPO)

banking_stability.notes: A small, shallow, foreign-bank-influenced system with limited credit penetration - stable but undeveloped. (IMF 2024)

trade_actions_capacity.notes: A dangerously narrow, depleting base - over-harvested logging plus fish dominate exports, with new Chinese loan dependency. (IMF 2024; Mongabay)

institutional.notes: Weak rule of law and corruption, with a stark Honiara-vs-provinces capacity gap.

rule_of_law.notes: Generally respected rights but weak rule of law, corruption (CPI 43/100), and logging-permit abuse; the May 2026 courts forced a delayed no-confidence vote - a positive signal. (Freedom House 2025; TI 2024)

statistical_integrity.notes: Weak, donor-dependent capacity, with sparse and lagged figures. (IMF; World Bank)

civil_service_capacity.notes: Limited reach with a stark Honiara-vs-provinces service gap - a root cause of the Tensions and ongoing Malaita grievance. (USIP 2024; CRS)

press_freedom.notes: A relatively pluralistic but pressured environment (the Sogavare government floated media controls); not RSF-ranked, so confidence is capped. (Freedom House 2025; CIVICUS)

political_social.notes: Democracy held through brinkmanship, over a live Tensions/anti-China fault line.

political_stability.notes: A peaceful 2024 election was followed by an eight-week 2026 constitutional crisis the Court of Appeal had to resolve (ousting Manele, installing Wale) - democracy held but through brinkmanship, and the new government is untested. (RFA; Devpolicy 2026)

civil_liberties.notes: Generally respected speech/assembly/religion, with periodic restrictions during unrest. (Freedom House 2025; CIVICUS)

treatment_non_citizens.notes: No systematic exit-ban regime, but the 2021 riots specifically targeted the Chinese community/Chinatown - a demonstrated risk. (RNZ; CNN 2021)

social_cohesion.notes: The Malaita/Guadalcanal 'Tensions' legacy (RAMSI 2003-2017) and 2021 anti-China riots show a live fault line tied to the China alignment and provincial inequality. (USIP 2024; Mongabay)

demographics.notes: Young, fast-growing, rapidly urbanizing - pressuring scarce Honiara services and jobs, a contributor to unrest. (World Bank; UN DESA)

geopolitical.notes: A volatile multi-aligned 'friends to all', leader-dependent and unpredictable.

alliance_reliability.notes: A volatile multi-alignment - the 2019 China switch and 2022 pact alarmed the West, but the May 2026 arrival of pact-critic Wale (plus a 2024 Australian RSIPF deal) suggests a Western tilt; unpredictable and leader-dependent. (Lowy 2026; ASPI; CRS)

conflict_involvement.notes: No external conflict; the risk is recurrent internal unrest (2021 riots) and great-power basing friction. (CFR; Lowy)

sanctions_capital_controls.notes: Unsanctioned and open, with routine small-economy exchange administration. (IMF 2024)

physical_practical.notes: Among the world's most climate-vulnerable, with weak health and dispersed-archipelago infrastructure.

climate.notes: Among the world's most climate-vulnerable - documented disappearance of low-lying reef islands, sea-level rise, cyclones, and king tides. (UNEP; peer-reviewed studies)

healthcare.notes: A weak, rural-access-poor system flagged as a top election issue, reliant on foreign (incl. Chinese) medical support. (PIReport 2025; WHO)

infrastructure.notes: Poor roads, limited grid/water/broadband, and dispersed-archipelago logistics; the pipeline is heavily donor/loan-financed. (ADB; World Bank)

crime_safety.notes: Lower routine violent crime than PNG but recurrent mob/riot risk (2021) and rising urban problems. (CIVICUS; Freedom House 2025)

disaster_insurance.notes: Negligible penetration against extreme climate and seismic/tsunami exposure, partly offset by regional pooling (PCRIC). (inference; PCRIC)

summary: A small, aid-dependent, logging-driven economy that became a great-power flashpoint after its 2019 switch from Taiwan to China and the controversial 2022 security pact, then swung again in May 2026 when PM Manele was ousted and pact-critic Matthew Wale became prime minister - a likely tilt back toward Australia, though the new government is untested and the transition came only after an eight-week constitutional crisis the courts had to resolve. Fiscally it is fragile rather than insolvent: public debt is low (~20% of GDP) but has tripled since the pandemic while the logging revenue base depletes. Deep structural risks persist - the Malaita/Guadalcanal 'Tensions' legacy, the 2021 anti-China Honiara riots, weak health and infrastructure, and acute climate vulnerability (low-lying islands literally disappearing). The skew is negative: democracy held and the West-tilting leadership change is a near-term positive, but it sits atop chronic instability, resource depletion, and existential climate exposure.
